sourceRefs.fallbackText: 7. Input and Output
sourceRefs.textQuote: 7. Input and Output

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Theme Auto Light Dark |
sourceRefs.textQuote: Theme Auto Light Dark |

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are several ways to present the output of a program; data can be printed in a human-readable form, or written to a file for future use. This chapter will discuss some of the possibilities.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are several ways to present the output of a program; data can be printed in a human-readable form, or written to a file for future use. This chapter will discuss some of the possibilities.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: So far we’ve encountered two ways of writing values: expression statements and the print() function. (A third way is using the write() method of file objects; the standard output file can be referenced as sys.stdout . See the Library Reference for more information on this.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: So far we’ve encountered two ways of writing values: expression statements and the print() function. (A third way is using the write() method of file objects; the standard output file can be referenced as sys.stdout . Se

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Often you’ll want more control over the formatting of your output than simply printing space-separated values. There are several ways to format output.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Often you’ll want more control over the formatting of your output than simply printing space-separated values. There are several ways to format output.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To use formatted string literals , begin a string with f or F before the opening quotation mark or triple quotation mark. Inside this string, you can write a Python expression between { and } characters that can refer to variables or literal values.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To use formatted string literals , begin a string with f or F before the opening quotation mark or triple quotation mark. Inside this string, you can write a Python expression between { and } characters that can refer to

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> year = 2016 >>> event = 'Referendum' >>> f 'Results of the { year } { event } ' 'Results of the 2016 Referendum'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> year = 2016 >>> event = 'Referendum' >>> f 'Results of the { year } { event } ' 'Results of the 2016 Referendum'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The str.format() method of strings requires more manual effort. You’ll still use { and } to mark where a variable will be substituted and can provide detailed formatting directives, but you’ll also need to provide the information to be formatted. In the following code block there are two examples of how to format variables:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The str.format() method of strings requires more manual effort. You’ll still use { and } to mark where a variable will be substituted and can provide detailed formatting directives, but you’ll also need to provide the in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> yes_votes = 42_572_654 >>> total_votes = 85_705_149 >>> percentage = yes_votes / total_votes >>> ' {:-9} YES votes {:2.2%} ' . format ( yes_votes , percentage ) ' 42572654 YES votes 49.67%'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> yes_votes = 42_572_654 >>> total_votes = 85_705_149 >>> percentage = yes_votes / total_votes >>> ' {:-9} YES votes {:2.2%} ' . format ( yes_votes , percentage ) ' 42572654 YES votes 49.67%'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Notice how the yes_votes are padded with spaces and a negative sign only for negative numbers. The example also prints percentage multiplied by 100, with 2 decimal places and followed by a percent sign (see Format specification mini-language for details).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Notice how the yes_votes are padded with spaces and a negative sign only for negative numbers. The example also prints percentage multiplied by 100, with 2 decimal places and followed by a percent sign (see Format specif

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Finally, you can do all the string handling yourself by using string slicing and concatenation operations to create any layout you can imagine. The string type has some methods that perform useful operations for padding strings to a given column width.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Finally, you can do all the string handling yourself by using string slicing and concatenation operations to create any layout you can imagine. The string type has some methods that perform useful operations for padding

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When you don’t need fancy output but just want a quick display of some variables for debugging purposes, you can convert any value to a string with the repr() or str() functions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: When you don’t need fancy output but just want a quick display of some variables for debugging purposes, you can convert any value to a string with the repr() or str() functions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The str() function is meant to return representations of values which are fairly human-readable, while repr() is meant to generate representations which can be read by the interpreter (or will force a SyntaxError if there is no equivalent syntax). For objects which don’t have a particular representation for human consumption, str() will return the same value
sourceRefs.textQuote: The str() function is meant to return representations of values which are fairly human-readable, while repr() is meant to generate representations which can be read by the interpreter (or will force a SyntaxError if ther

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> s = 'Hello, world.' >>> str ( s ) 'Hello, world.' >>> repr ( s ) "'Hello, world.'" >>> str ( 1 / 7 ) '0.14285714285714285' >>> x = 10 * 3.25 >>> y = 200 * 200 >>> s = 'The value of x is ' + repr ( x ) + ', and y is ' + repr ( y ) + '...' >>> print ( s ) The value of x is 32.5, and y is 40000... >>> # The repr() of a string adds string quotes and backslas
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> s = 'Hello, world.' >>> str ( s ) 'Hello, world.' >>> repr ( s ) "'Hello, world.'" >>> str ( 1 / 7 ) '0.14285714285714285' >>> x = 10 * 3.25 >>> y = 200 * 200 >>> s = 'The value of x is ' + repr ( x ) + ', and y is '

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The string module contains support for a simple templating approach based upon regular expressions, via string.Template . This offers yet another way to substitute values into strings, using placeholders like $x and replacing them with values from a dictionary. This syntax is easy to use, although it offers much less control for formatting.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The string module contains support for a simple templating approach based upon regular expressions, via string.Template . This offers yet another way to substitute values into strings, using placeholders like $x and repl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Formatted string literals (also called f-strings for short) let you include the value of Python expressions inside a string by prefixing the string with f or F and writing expressions as {expression} .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Formatted string literals (also called f-strings for short) let you include the value of Python expressions inside a string by prefixing the string with f or F and writing expressions as {expression} .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An optional format specifier can follow the expression. This allows greater control over how the value is formatted. The following example rounds pi to three places after the decimal:
sourceRefs.textQuote: An optional format specifier can follow the expression. This allows greater control over how the value is formatted. The following example rounds pi to three places after the decimal:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> import math >>> print ( f 'The value of pi is approximately { math . pi : .3f } .' ) The value of pi is approximately 3.142.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> import math >>> print ( f 'The value of pi is approximately { math . pi : .3f } .' ) The value of pi is approximately 3.142.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Passing an integer after the ':' will cause that field to be a minimum number of characters wide. This is useful for making columns line up.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Passing an integer after the ':' will cause that field to be a minimum number of characters wide. This is useful for making columns line up.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 7678 } >>> for name , phone in table . items (): ... print ( f ' { name : 10 } ==> { phone : 10d } ' ) ... Sjoerd ==> 4127 Jack ==> 4098 Dcab ==> 7678
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 7678 } >>> for name , phone in table . items (): ... print ( f ' { name : 10 } ==> { phone : 10d } ' ) ... Sjoerd ==> 4127 Jack ==> 4098 Dcab ==> 7678

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Other modifiers can be used to convert the value before it is formatted. '!a' applies ascii() , '!s' applies str() , and '!r' applies repr() :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Other modifiers can be used to convert the value before it is formatted. '!a' applies ascii() , '!s' applies str() , and '!r' applies repr() :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> animals = 'eels' >>> print ( f 'My hovercraft is full of { animals } .' ) My hovercraft is full of eels. >>> print ( f 'My hovercraft is full of { animals !r} .' ) My hovercraft is full of 'eels'.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> animals = 'eels' >>> print ( f 'My hovercraft is full of { animals } .' ) My hovercraft is full of eels. >>> print ( f 'My hovercraft is full of { animals !r} .' ) My hovercraft is full of 'eels'.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The = specifier can be used to expand an expression to the text of the expression, an equal sign, then the representation of the evaluated expression:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The = specifier can be used to expand an expression to the text of the expression, an equal sign, then the representation of the evaluated expression:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> bugs = 'roaches' >>> count = 13 >>> area = 'living room' >>> print ( f 'Debugging { bugs =} { count =} { area =} ' ) Debugging bugs='roaches' count=13 area='living room'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> bugs = 'roaches' >>> count = 13 >>> area = 'living room' >>> print ( f 'Debugging { bugs =} { count =} { area =} ' ) Debugging bugs='roaches' count=13 area='living room'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: See self-documenting expressions for more information on the = specifier. For a reference on these format specifications, see the reference guide for the Format specification mini-language .
sourceRefs.textQuote: See self-documenting expressions for more information on the = specifier. For a reference on these format specifications, see the reference guide for the Format specification mini-language .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Basic usage of the str.format() method looks like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Basic usage of the str.format() method looks like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print ( 'We are the {} who say " {} !"' . format ( 'knights' , 'Ni' )) We are the knights who say "Ni!"
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print ( 'We are the {} who say " {} !"' . format ( 'knights' , 'Ni' )) We are the knights who say "Ni!"

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The brackets and characters within them (called format fields) are replaced with the objects passed into the str.format() method. A number in the brackets can be used to refer to the position of the object passed into the str.format() method.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The brackets and characters within them (called format fields) are replaced with the objects passed into the str.format() method. A number in the brackets can be used to refer to the position of the object passed into th

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print ( ' {0} and {1} ' . format ( 'spam' , 'eggs' )) spam and eggs >>> print ( ' {1} and {0} ' . format ( 'spam' , 'eggs' )) eggs and spam
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print ( ' {0} and {1} ' . format ( 'spam' , 'eggs' )) spam and eggs >>> print ( ' {1} and {0} ' . format ( 'spam' , 'eggs' )) eggs and spam

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If keyword arguments are used in the str.format() method, their values are referred to by using the name of the argument.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If keyword arguments are used in the str.format() method, their values are referred to by using the name of the argument.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print ( 'This {food} is {adjective} .' . format ( ... food = 'spam' , adjective = 'absolutely horrible' )) This spam is absolutely horrible.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print ( 'This {food} is {adjective} .' . format ( ... food = 'spam' , adjective = 'absolutely horrible' )) This spam is absolutely horrible.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Positional and keyword arguments can be arbitrarily combined:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Positional and keyword arguments can be arbitrarily combined:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print ( 'The story of {0} , {1} , and {other} .' . format ( 'Bill' , 'Manfred' , ... other = 'Georg' )) The story of Bill, Manfred, and Georg.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print ( 'The story of {0} , {1} , and {other} .' . format ( 'Bill' , 'Manfred' , ... other = 'Georg' )) The story of Bill, Manfred, and Georg.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you have a really long format string that you don’t want to split up, it would be nice if you could reference the variables to be formatted by name instead of by position. This can be done by simply passing the dict and using square brackets '[]' to access the keys.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you have a really long format string that you don’t want to split up, it would be nice if you could reference the variables to be formatted by name instead of by position. This can be done by simply passing the dict a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 8637678 } >>> print ( 'Jack: {0[Jack]:d} ; Sjoerd: {0[Sjoerd]:d} ; ' ... 'Dcab: {0[Dcab]:d} ' . format ( table )) Jack: 4098; Sjoerd: 4127; Dcab: 8637678
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 8637678 } >>> print ( 'Jack: {0[Jack]:d} ; Sjoerd: {0[Sjoerd]:d} ; ' ... 'Dcab: {0[Dcab]:d} ' . format ( table )) Jack: 4098; Sjoerd: 4127; Dcab: 8637678

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This could also be done by passing the table dictionary as keyword arguments with the ** notation.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This could also be done by passing the table dictionary as keyword arguments with the ** notation.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 8637678 } >>> print ( 'Jack: {Jack:d} ; Sjoerd: {Sjoerd:d} ; Dcab: {Dcab:d} ' . format ( ** table )) Jack: 4098; Sjoerd: 4127; Dcab: 8637678
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> table = { 'Sjoerd' : 4127 , 'Jack' : 4098 , 'Dcab' : 8637678 } >>> print ( 'Jack: {Jack:d} ; Sjoerd: {Sjoerd:d} ; Dcab: {Dcab:d} ' . format ( ** table )) Jack: 4098; Sjoerd: 4127; Dcab: 8637678

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is particularly useful in combination with the built-in function vars() , which returns a dictionary containing all local variables:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This is particularly useful in combination with the built-in function vars() , which returns a dictionary containing all local variables:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> table = { k : str ( v ) for k , v in vars () . items ()} >>> message = " " . join ([ f ' { k } : ' + '{' + k + '};' for k in table . keys ()]) >>> print ( message . format ( ** table )) __name__: __main__; __doc__: None; __package__: None; __loader__: ...
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> table = { k : str ( v ) for k , v in vars () . items ()} >>> message = " " . join ([ f ' { k } : ' + '{' + k + '};' for k in table . keys ()]) >>> print ( message . format ( ** table )) __name__: __main__; __doc__: N

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As an example, the following lines produce a tidily aligned set of columns giving integers and their squares and cubes:
sourceRefs.textQuote: As an example, the following lines produce a tidily aligned set of columns giving integers and their squares and cubes:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For a complete overview of string formatting with str.format() , see Format string syntax .
sourceRefs.textQuote: For a complete overview of string formatting with str.format() , see Format string syntax .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here’s the same table of squares and cubes, formatted manually:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here’s the same table of squares and cubes, formatted manually:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> for x in range ( 1 , 11 ): ... print ( repr ( x ) . rjust ( 2 ), repr ( x * x ) . rjust ( 3 ), end = ' ' ) ... # Note use of 'end' on previous line ... print ( repr ( x * x * x ) . rjust ( 4 )) ... 1 1 1 2 4 8 3 9 27 4 16 64 5 25 125 6 36 216 7 49 343 8 64 512 9 81 729 10 100 1000
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> for x in range ( 1 , 11 ): ... print ( repr ( x ) . rjust ( 2 ), repr ( x * x ) . rjust ( 3 ), end = ' ' ) ... # Note use of 'end' on previous line ... print ( repr ( x * x * x ) . rjust ( 4 )) ... 1 1 1 2 4 8 3 9 27

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (Note that the one space between each column was added by the way print() works: it always adds spaces between its arguments.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (Note that the one space between each column was added by the way print() works: it always adds spaces between its arguments.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The str.rjust() method of string objects right-justifies a string in a field of a given width by padding it with spaces on the left. There are similar methods str.ljust() and str.center() . These methods do not write anything, they just return a new string. If the input string is too long, they don’t truncate it, but return it unchanged; this will mess up yo
sourceRefs.textQuote: The str.rjust() method of string objects right-justifies a string in a field of a given width by padding it with spaces on the left. There are similar methods str.ljust() and str.center() . These methods do not write any

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is another method, str.zfill() , which pads a numeric string on the left with zeros. It understands about plus and minus signs:
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is another method, str.zfill() , which pads a numeric string on the left with zeros. It understands about plus and minus signs:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The % operator (modulo) can also be used for string formatting. Given format % values (where format is a string), % conversion specifications in format are replaced with zero or more elements of values . This operation is commonly known as string interpolation. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The % operator (modulo) can also be used for string formatting. Given format % values (where format is a string), % conversion specifications in format are replaced with zero or more elements of values . This operation i

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> import math >>> print ( 'The value of pi is approximately %5.3f .' % math . pi ) The value of pi is approximately 3.142.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> import math >>> print ( 'The value of pi is approximately %5.3f .' % math . pi ) The value of pi is approximately 3.142.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: More information can be found in the printf-style String Formatting section.
sourceRefs.textQuote: More information can be found in the printf-style String Formatting section.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: open() returns a file object , and is most commonly used with two positional arguments and one keyword argument: open(filename, mode, encoding=None)
sourceRefs.textQuote: open() returns a file object , and is most commonly used with two positional arguments and one keyword argument: open(filename, mode, encoding=None)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f = open ( 'workfile' , 'w' , encoding = "utf-8" )
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f = open ( 'workfile' , 'w' , encoding = "utf-8" )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The first argument is a string containing the filename. The second argument is another string containing a few characters describing the way in which the file will be used. mode can be 'r' when the file will only be read, 'w' for only writing (an existing file with the same name will be erased), and 'a' opens the file for appending; any data written to the f
sourceRefs.textQuote: The first argument is a string containing the filename. The second argument is another string containing a few characters describing the way in which the file will be used. mode can be 'r' when the file will only be read

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Normally, files are opened in text mode , that means, you read and write strings from and to the file, which are encoded in a specific encoding . If encoding is not specified, the default is platform dependent (see open() ). Because UTF-8 is the modern de-facto standard, encoding="utf-8" is recommended unless you know that you need to use a different encodin
sourceRefs.textQuote: Normally, files are opened in text mode , that means, you read and write strings from and to the file, which are encoded in a specific encoding . If encoding is not specified, the default is platform dependent (see open(

sourceRefs.fallbackText:
In text mode, the default when reading is to convert platform-specific line endings ( 
 on Unix, 
 on Windows) to just 
 . When writing in text mode, the default is to convert occurrences of 
 back to platform-specific line endings. This behind-the-scenes modification to file data is fine for text files, but will corrupt binary data like that in JPEG o
sourceRefs.textQuote:
In text mode, the default when reading is to convert platform-specific line endings ( 
 on Unix, 
 on Windows) to just 
 . When writing in text mode, the default is to convert occurrences of 
 back to platform-spec

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is good practice to use the with keyword when dealing with file objects. The advantage is that the file is properly closed after its suite finishes, even if an exception is raised at some point. Using with is also much shorter than writing equivalent try - finally blocks:
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is good practice to use the with keyword when dealing with file objects. The advantage is that the file is properly closed after its suite finishes, even if an exception is raised at some point. Using with is also muc

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> with open ( 'workfile' , encoding = "utf-8" ) as f : ... read_data = f . read () >>> # We can check that the file has been automatically closed. >>> f . closed True
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> with open ( 'workfile' , encoding = "utf-8" ) as f : ... read_data = f . read () >>> # We can check that the file has been automatically closed. >>> f . closed True

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you’re not using the with keyword, then you should call f.close() to close the file and immediately free up any system resources used by it.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you’re not using the with keyword, then you should call f.close() to close the file and immediately free up any system resources used by it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Calling f.write() without using the with keyword or calling f.close() might result in the arguments of f.write() not being completely written to the disk, even if the program exits successfully.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Calling f.write() without using the with keyword or calling f.close() might result in the arguments of f.write() not being completely written to the disk, even if the program exits successfully.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: After a file object is closed, either by a with statement or by calling f.close() , attempts to use the file object will automatically fail.
sourceRefs.textQuote: After a file object is closed, either by a with statement or by calling f.close() , attempts to use the file object will automatically fail.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f . close () >>> f . read () Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> ValueError : I/O operation on closed file.
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f . close () >>> f . read () Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> ValueError : I/O operation on closed file.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The rest of the examples in this section will assume that a file object called f has already been created.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The rest of the examples in this section will assume that a file object called f has already been created.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To read a file’s contents, call f.read(size) , which reads some quantity of data and returns it as a string (in text mode) or bytes object (in binary mode). size is an optional numeric argument. When size is omitted or negative, the entire contents of the file will be read and returned; it’s your problem if the file is twice as large as your machine’s memory
sourceRefs.textQuote: To read a file’s contents, call f.read(size) , which reads some quantity of data and returns it as a string (in text mode) or bytes object (in binary mode). size is an optional numeric argument. When size is omitted or n

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f . read () 'This is the entire file.\n' >>> f . read () ''
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f . read () 'This is the entire file.\n' >>> f . read () ''

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f.readline() reads a single line from the file; a newline character ( \n ) is left at the end of the string, and is only omitted on the last line of the file if the file doesn’t end in a newline. This makes the return value unambiguous; if f.readline() returns an empty string, the end of the file has been reached, while a blank line is represented by '\n' ,
sourceRefs.textQuote: f.readline() reads a single line from the file; a newline character ( \n ) is left at the end of the string, and is only omitted on the last line of the file if the file doesn’t end in a newline. This makes the return va

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f . readline () 'This is the first line of the file.\n' >>> f . readline () 'Second line of the file\n' >>> f . readline () ''
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f . readline () 'This is the first line of the file.\n' >>> f . readline () 'Second line of the file\n' >>> f . readline () ''

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For reading lines from a file, you can loop over the file object. This is memory efficient, fast, and leads to simple code:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For reading lines from a file, you can loop over the file object. This is memory efficient, fast, and leads to simple code:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> for line in f : ... print ( line , end = '' ) ... This is the first line of the file. Second line of the file
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> for line in f : ... print ( line , end = '' ) ... This is the first line of the file. Second line of the file

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you want to read all the lines of a file in a list you can also use list(f) or f.readlines() .
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you want to read all the lines of a file in a list you can also use list(f) or f.readlines() .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f.write(string) writes the contents of string to the file, returning the number of characters written.
sourceRefs.textQuote: f.write(string) writes the contents of string to the file, returning the number of characters written.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f . write ( 'This is a test \n ' ) 15
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f . write ( 'This is a test \n ' ) 15

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Other types of objects need to be converted – either to a string (in text mode) or a bytes object (in binary mode) – before writing them:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Other types of objects need to be converted – either to a string (in text mode) or a bytes object (in binary mode) – before writing them:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> value = ( 'the answer' , 42 ) >>> s = str ( value ) # convert the tuple to string >>> f . write ( s ) 18
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> value = ( 'the answer' , 42 ) >>> s = str ( value ) # convert the tuple to string >>> f . write ( s ) 18

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f.tell() returns an integer giving the file object’s current position in the file represented as number of bytes from the beginning of the file when in binary mode and an opaque number when in text mode.
sourceRefs.textQuote: f.tell() returns an integer giving the file object’s current position in the file represented as number of bytes from the beginning of the file when in binary mode and an opaque number when in text mode.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To change the file object’s position, use f.seek(offset, whence) . The position is computed from adding offset to a reference point; the reference point is selected by the whence argument. A whence value of 0 measures from the beginning of the file, 1 uses the current file position, and 2 uses the end of the file as the reference point. whence can be omitted
sourceRefs.textQuote: To change the file object’s position, use f.seek(offset, whence) . The position is computed from adding offset to a reference point; the reference point is selected by the whence argument. A whence value of 0 measures fr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> f = open ( 'workfile' , 'rb+' ) >>> f . write ( b '0123456789abcdef' ) 16 >>> f . seek ( 5 ) # Go to the 6th byte in the file 5 >>> f . read ( 1 ) b'5' >>> f . seek ( - 3 , 2 ) # Go to the 3rd byte before the end 13 >>> f . read ( 1 ) b'd'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> f = open ( 'workfile' , 'rb+' ) >>> f . write ( b '0123456789abcdef' ) 16 >>> f . seek ( 5 ) # Go to the 6th byte in the file 5 >>> f . read ( 1 ) b'5' >>> f . seek ( - 3 , 2 ) # Go to the 3rd byte before the end 13

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In text files (those opened without a b in the mode string), only seeks relative to the beginning of the file are allowed (the exception being seeking to the very file end with seek(0, 2) ) and the only valid offset values are those returned from the f.tell() , or zero. Any other offset value produces undefined behaviour.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In text files (those opened without a b in the mode string), only seeks relative to the beginning of the file are allowed (the exception being seeking to the very file end with seek(0, 2) ) and the only valid offset valu

sourceRefs.fallbackText: File objects have some additional methods, such as isatty() and truncate() which are less frequently used; consult the Library Reference for a complete guide to file objects.
sourceRefs.textQuote: File objects have some additional methods, such as isatty() and truncate() which are less frequently used; consult the Library Reference for a complete guide to file objects.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Strings can easily be written to and read from a file. Numbers take a bit more effort, since the read() method only returns strings, which will have to be passed to a function like int() , which takes a string like '123' and returns its numeric value 123. When you want to save more complex data types like nested lists and dictionaries, parsing and serializin
sourceRefs.textQuote: Strings can easily be written to and read from a file. Numbers take a bit more effort, since the read() method only returns strings, which will have to be passed to a function like int() , which takes a string like '123'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Rather than having users constantly writing and debugging code to save complicated data types to files, Python allows you to use the popular data interchange format called JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) . The standard module called json can take Python data hierarchies, and convert them to string representations; this process is called serializing . Recon
sourceRefs.textQuote: Rather than having users constantly writing and debugging code to save complicated data types to files, Python allows you to use the popular data interchange format called JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) . The standard

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The JSON format is commonly used by modern applications to allow for data exchange. Many programmers are already familiar with it, which makes it a good choice for interoperability.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The JSON format is commonly used by modern applications to allow for data exchange. Many programmers are already familiar with it, which makes it a good choice for interoperability.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you have an object x , you can view its JSON string representation with a simple line of code:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you have an object x , you can view its JSON string representation with a simple line of code:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> import json >>> x = [ 1 , 'simple' , 'list' ] >>> json . dumps ( x ) '[1, "simple", "list"]'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> import json >>> x = [ 1 , 'simple' , 'list' ] >>> json . dumps ( x ) '[1, "simple", "list"]'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another variant of the dumps() function, called dump() , simply serializes the object to a text file . So if f is a text file object opened for writing, we can do this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another variant of the dumps() function, called dump() , simply serializes the object to a text file . So if f is a text file object opened for writing, we can do this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: json . dump ( x , f )
sourceRefs.textQuote: json . dump ( x , f )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To decode the object again, if f is a binary file or text file object which has been opened for reading:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To decode the object again, if f is a binary file or text file object which has been opened for reading:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: x = json . load ( f )
sourceRefs.textQuote: x = json . load ( f )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: JSON files must be encoded in UTF-8. Use encoding="utf-8" when opening JSON file as a text file for both of reading and writing.
sourceRefs.textQuote: JSON files must be encoded in UTF-8. Use encoding="utf-8" when opening JSON file as a text file for both of reading and writing.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This simple serialization technique can handle lists and dictionaries, but serializing arbitrary class instances in JSON requires a bit of extra effort. The reference for the json module contains an explanation of this.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This simple serialization technique can handle lists and dictionaries, but serializing arbitrary class instances in JSON requires a bit of extra effort. The reference for the json module contains an explanation of this.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: pickle - the pickle module
sourceRefs.textQuote: pickle - the pickle module

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Contrary to JSON , pickle is a protocol which allows the serialization of arbitrarily complex Python objects. As such, it is specific to Python and cannot be used to communicate with applications written in other languages. It is also insecure by default: deserializing pickle data coming from an untrusted source can execute arbitrary code, if the data was cr
sourceRefs.textQuote: Contrary to JSON , pickle is a protocol which allows the serialization of arbitrarily complex Python objects. As such, it is specific to Python and cannot be used to communicate with applications written in other languag
